Babylon is represented as a female in deep distress. She was destined to be degraded and endure suffering; she is shown sitting on the ground, grinding at the handmill, which is the lowest and most laborious service. God was righteous in his vengeance, and no one should interpose. The prophet rejoices in the Lord of hosts as the Redeemer and Holy One of Israel. God often permits wicked men to prevail against his people; but those who cruelly oppress them will be punished.

Let us beware of acting and speaking as Babylon did; of trusting in tyranny and oppression; of boasting about our abilities, relying on ourselves, and attributing success to our own prudence and wisdom; lest we share in her plagues. Those experiencing great prosperity are inclined to think they are beyond adversity. It is also common for sinners to believe they will be safe because they try to be secret in their wicked ways. But their security will lead to their ruin. Let us draw from passages like the one above those lessons of humility and trust in God which they convey. If we believe the word of God, we may know how it will be with the righteous and the wicked for all eternity. We may learn how to escape the coming wrath, to glorify God, to have peace through life, hope in death, and everlasting happiness. Let us then stand aloof from all delusions.
